Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Igs Solutions Llc Medical Group, Inc. To Further Respond To Set One Of Plaintiff'S Form Interrogatories - General, And Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $3,575.00; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; Declaration Of Elen Martirosyan
Off Calendar Feb-03-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF MARIAH MCBRIDE's Motion To Compel Defendant Igs Solutions Llc Medical Group, Inc. To Further Respond To Set One Of Plaintiff'S Form Interrogatories - General, And Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $3,575.00.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Mariah McBride's motion to compel further responses to special interrogatories set 1 and employment form interrogatories set 1, and for sanctions, is granted in part and denied in part.

With respect to form interrogatory 209.2, the motion is granted. McBride has equal access to court documents and records, but not equal access to information about which cases contain those records. Defendant IGS Solutions LLC's objection is overruled and IGS is ordered to amend its response to this interrogatory within 10 court days of the date of notice of entry of this order. The court finds McBride's meet and confer efforts were sufficient as to this interrogatory.

With respect to special interrogatories 36 through 126, the court denies the motion to compel further responses for McBride's failure to meet and confer adequately under all the facts presented here.

CCP 2030.040 permits a party to propound additional special interrogatories beyond 35 with contemporaneous service of a supporting declaration containing substantially the language of CCP 2030.050. Once a propounding party serves that supporting declaration, the responding party is obliged to respond, "[s]ubject to the right of the responding party to seek a protective order under Section 2030.090." (CCP 2030.040(a).) In other words, the responding party may seek a protective order to avoid responding, or it may respond, including by asserting objections.

The supporting declaration here was substantially in the form prescribed by CCP 2030.050. While it was boilerplate, it complied with the language of the statute. IGS responded by submitted only objections, contending that the declaration was insufficient to require it to provide a substantive response. In meet and confer efforts, just before the motion to compel deadline expired, IGS clarified that it was seeking "justification based on the needs of this case." (Ex. 9 to Gehsgian Dec.) IGS would not extend the deadline on McBride's motion to compel, and McBride did not provide justification.

CCP 2030.050 states in part that a declaration in support of additional interrogatories should include the following language: "8. This number of questions is warranted under Section 2030.040 of the Code of Civil Procedure because __________. (Here state each factor described in Section 2030.040 that is relied on, as well as the reasons why any factor relied on is applicable to the instant lawsuit.)" IGS's objections essentially pointed out that McBride had not done so sufficiently in this case. McBride's meet and confer response was essentially to insist that the language of the declaration was enough. Restating a position is not a "reasonable and good faith attempt" to resolve discovery issues informally. (CCP 2016.040, 2030.300(b).) Such a good faith attempt could have entailed explaining in further detail why the additional interrogatories were warranted; that did not occur here. In view of the volume of discovery served in this case, and in light of all the facts set out in the parties' declarations, something more was required here, and the court finds McBride's meet and confer attempts to be inadequate.

Both parties seek sanctions. The court finds that McBride did not act with substantial justification and awards $1000 sanctions payable by McBride to IGS within 10 court days of the date of entry of this order.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
